The Maryland Port Administration (MPA) promotes efforts to increase waterborne commerce in the State and manages the State-owned facilities at the Helen Delich Bentley Port of Baltimore (Port).
The temporary closure of the Port in fiscal 2024 resulted in overall declines in general cargo of about 21.3% compared to fiscal 2023 totals, from 11.6 million tons to 9.1 million tons.
The fiscal 2027 Budget Bill contains a proposed deficiency appropriation for fiscal 2026 for MPA totaling $681,198 in special funds for software licenses."},{"url":"https://conduitstreet.mdcounties.org/2025/12/29/2026-issue-preview-chesapeake-bay-restoration/","title":"2026 Issue Preview: Chesapeake Bay Restoration – Conduit Street","content":"Moreover, Maryland’s Phase III WIP acknowledges that pollution loading resulting from climate change, population growth, and the Conowingo Dam may impact the achievement and sustainability of restoration beyond 2025.
Trump’s federal fiscal 2026 budget request recommends maintaining CBP funding at $92 million, consistent with appropriations in recent years."},{"url":"https://governor.maryland.gov/news/press-releases/governor-wes-moore-announces-315-million-ecological-restoration-projects","title":"Governor Wes Moore Announces $31.5 Million for Ecological Restoration Projects to Improve Water Quality in Local Waterways and the Chesapeake Bay | The Office of Governor Wes Moore","content":"ANNAPOLIS, MD – Governor Wes Moore today announced the Maryland Department of Natural Resources is awarding $31.5 million in grants from the Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bays Trust Fund for 25 ecological restoration projects encompassing 188 sites throughout the state that will improve water quality and wildlife habitats.
“The Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bay Trust Fund grants enable DNR to work directly with community organizations, local governments, and scientific experts to complete projects that will result in lasting improvements to the Coastal Bays, the Chesapeake Bay, and their watersheds throughout Maryland.”
The City of Frederick will restore 7,580 linear feet of the Carroll Creek watershed and plant 24,000 trees resulting in water quality and flooding improvements as well as a passive park for recreation."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Mid-Chesapeake Bay Environmental Restoration Project Maryland Port Administration federal funding amount","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://governor.maryland.gov/news/press-releases/governor-wes-moore-announces-315-million-ecological-restoration-projects","title":"Governor Wes Moore Announces $31.5 Million for Ecological Restoration Projects to Improve Water Quality in Local Waterways and the Chesapeake Bay | The Office of Governor Wes Moore","content":"An official website of the State of Maryland. # Governor Wes Moore Announces $31.5 Million for Ecological Restoration Projects to Improve Water Quality in Local Waterways and the Chesapeake Bay. **ANNAPOLIS, MD –** Governor Wes Moore today announced the Maryland Department of Natural Resources is awarding $31.5 million in grants from the Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bays Trust Fund for 25 ecological restoration projects encompassing 188 sites throughout the state that will improve water quality and wildlife habitats. “The Chesapeake Bay is the centerpiece of our tourism and seafood industries in Maryland,” **said Governor Moore**. “Strong partnerships are key to making significant progress on reducing Chesapeake Bay pollutants,” **said Maryland Secretary of Natural Resources Josh Kurtz.** “The Chesapeake and Atlantic Coastal Bay Trust Fund grants enable DNR to work directly with community organizations, local governments, and scientific experts to complete projects that will result in lasting improvements to the Coastal Bays, the Chesapeake Bay, and their watersheds throughout Maryland.”.","score":0.5752307,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.chesapeakebay.net/who/funding-and-financing","title":"Our Funding","content":"Funding for restoration efforts comes from numerous federal agencies, state and local governments, non-governmental organizations and private interests. Numerous federal agencies, state and local governments, non-governmental organizations and private interests financially support the restoration effort described in the commitments of the *Chesapeake Bay Watershed Agreement*. Funding provided through the Chesapeake Bay Program is a key factor in leveraging funds from a variety of sources and facilitates the efforts of many partners. Since the Chesapeake Bay program formed in 1983, several written agreements have guided the partnership's efforts to reduce pollution and restore the ecosystem. Close to two-thirds of the EPA funding for the Chesapeake Bay Program is provided directly to state and local partners for watershed restoration, protection and monitoring. Other federal agencies work closely together as part of the Chesapeake Bay Program to coordinate efforts and resources and interact with state partners.
